title: Proverbs 11
subtitle: Honest Scales; Pride and Humility; Community and Generosity

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — Honest Scales; Pride and Humility; Community Integrity

heb.paragraph: The opening verse condemns mōʾzĕnê mirmâ — scales rigged to cheat. In an agrarian economy, market scales determined survival. God's personal abhorrence (tôʿăbat yhwh) of commercial fraud reveals that economic justice is a theological issue, not merely a civil one.

With the ṣĕnûʿîm comes wisdom (v. 2). This rare word (appearing only here in the OT) describes those who are modest, unassuming, and unpretentious. Its contrast with zādôn (arrogance) reveals that wisdom requires a posture of receptivity — the arrogant cannot learn because they believe they already know.

refs.note: Do not use dishonest standards -- the legal basis for v.1.
Pride goes before destruction -- the same pride-disgrace sequence as v.2.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The opening section of ch.11 moves rapidly across economic ethics (scales, v.1), character (pride/humility, v.2), integrity in crisis (v.3–5), righteousness in death (v.4), and sexual ethics (adultery, v.6). Alter notes this variety is not random — the proverbs are illustrations of the same underlying principle: covenant faithfulness produces life; covenant violation produces destruction, regardless of the domain.

'The righteous person is rescued from trouble, and it falls on the wicked instead.' This lex talionis proverb — the trouble meant for the righteous falls on the wicked instead — is characteristic of Proverbs' worldview. Alter notes the reversal pattern (the righteous delivered; the wicked get what was aimed at the righteous) is a compressed theodicy: God's moral order inverts human injustice.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The honest-scales opening places covenant commerce at the heart of wisdom ethics. Waltke notes that dishonest weights were a form of economic violence against the poor and a theological offence against the God who is the standard of all measurement. The 'detestable' (to'evah) and 'delight' (ratson) language frames the economic ethics within the category of divine character — not merely illegal but an abomination.

'When pride comes, then comes disgrace, but with humility comes wisdom.' This is Proverbs' most direct statement of the pride-wisdom antithesis. Waltke notes 'pride' (zadon) is the arrogant self-assertion that refuses the epistemological submission that wisdom requires (3:5–7). Pride and wisdom are structurally incompatible: the proud person cannot receive wisdom because wisdom requires the acknowledgment of one's limitation.

Calvin: 'God has appointed equal weights for commerce not as a mere legal requirement but because he is himself the standard of all justice. To defraud with false weights is not merely to cheat a customer but to defy the God who weighs all things in perfect balance. The merchant who falsifies his scales declares that his standard is better than God's.'

Calvin on humility: 'Wisdom is given to the humble because they are the only ones who can receive it. The proud man is already convinced he knows — so he cannot learn. The humble man acknowledges he does not know — and so can be taught. Humility is not a virtue added to wisdom; it is the posture that makes wisdom possible.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'honest scales are the LORD's delight; dishonest scales are his abomination' (to'avat YHWH mozen mirmah veeven shelemah retsono) discusses the ancient Near Eastern background: merchants carried two sets of weights — heavier ones when buying (to pay less) and lighter ones when selling (to receive more). Lev 19:35–36 and Deut 25:13–16 also prohibit this. The 'abomination' language places economic fraud in the same moral category as idolatry.

The NET note on 'the righteousness of the blameless person keeps his way straight' discusses 'blameless' (tamim, complete/whole, the same word used of Job). The blameless person is not sinlessly perfect but whole in their orientation — fully committed to covenant faithfulness rather than divided between wisdom and folly.

hist.context: Proverbs 11 opens with the marketplace (v.1) and the central contrast: upright versus wicked. The city's prosperity or destruction depends on the moral quality of its residents (vv.10-11). Wisdom is not private virtue but a public good.

sections.header: Verses 16–31 — Generosity's Paradox; Beauty Without Discretion; Tree of Life

heb.paragraph: The fruit of the righteous is a 'tree of life' (v. 30) — a deliberate echo of Genesis 2–3. What was lost in Eden is partially recoverable through wisdom: life-giving influence that nourishes an entire community. The ʿēṣ ḥayyîm image recurs at 3:18, 13:12, and 15:4 as Proverbs' own creation-theology.
נֶפֶשׁ בְּרָכָה (nepeš bĕrākâ) = soul of blessing/generous soul

refs.note: The tree of life in Eden -- Proverbs reclaims this image for righteousness.
The one who sows generously will reap generously -- NT application of v.24's generous paradox.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The generosity paradox (vv.24–26) is one of Proverbs' most counter-intuitive observations. Alter notes its structure: three proverbs in a row that all point to the same reversal — giving produces increase; hoarding produces decrease; blessing on the generous; curses on the hoarder. The cluster creates an economic principle that works against human instinct and for divine economy.

The closing proverbs of ch.11 return to the household (trouble in one's own house, v.29) and the community (fruit of the righteous, tree of life, v.30) before the final observation: 'if the righteous receive their due on earth, how much more the ungodly and the sinner!' Alter notes the argument a fortiori — if even the righteous experience consequence, the wicked's consequences will be greater.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The second half of ch.11 develops the community-and-generosity theme. Waltke notes vv.24–26 form a paradox cluster: 'one person gives freely yet gains even more; another withholds unduly but comes to poverty.' This is Proverbs' most concentrated statement of generosity economics — the more you give, the more you have; the more you hoard, the less you have. It is both practical observation and theological claim about how God's moral order works.

'The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and the one who is wise saves lives.' The tree of life image (from Gen 2–3, explicitly) appears twice in Proverbs (11:30; 13:12; 15:4). Waltke notes Proverbs reclaims for wisdom the cosmic symbol of Eden's forbidden tree: wisdom produces what the unfaithful grasping of Genesis 3 sought to seize.

Calvin on generous giving: 'This is the economy of grace — it is contrary to carnal reason, which says that the more we give the less we have. But God's ordering reverses this: he who distributes liberally is enriched; he who hoards for fear of want impoverishes himself. The difference is not in the mathematics but in whether God is involved.'

Calvin on the tree of life: 'The righteous person's fruit is not merely beneficial but life-giving in the deepest sense — it communicates the life of God. The wise man who saves souls is imitating God, who is himself the source of all life. To cultivate wisdom in others is to participate in the divine work of giving life.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the liberality paradox ('one person gives freely yet gains even more') discusses the economic background: in ancient Israel, generous grain-lending during famines created social credit that was repaid multiply in good years. The 'gain even more' is not mystical multiplication but the social-economic reality of covenant community reciprocity.

The NET note on 'tree of life' (ets chayyim) traces the image from Gen 2:9 and 3:22–24 through Prov 3:18; 11:30; 13:12; 15:4 and into Rev 2:7; 22:2, 14. The tree of life is wisdom's symbol in Proverbs (3:18: 'she is a tree of life to those who take hold of her'). The righteous person whose life produces the tree of life is enacting what the original tree of life represents: access to the eternal life of the covenant God.

hist.context: The second half addresses the generous paradox (vv.24-26), wealth-trust (v.28), and the social fruitfulness of the righteous (vv.30-31). The gold-ring-in-a-pig's-snout image (v.22): beauty divorced from character is grotesque.

ppl.name: The upright
ppl.text: Their integrity is both compass and shield.

ppl.name: The generous person
ppl.text: Participates in the community's life-giving rhythms.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"One person gives freely, yet gains even more; another withholds unduly, but comes to poverty."NoteA description of moral ecology: open hands remain open to receive; closed fists close everything off.

src.title: Commercial weights
src.quote: Ancient merchants carried stone weights. Dishonest weights were a universal temptation.
src.note: Archaeological evidence confirms ubiquity of dishonest weights.

src.title: Tree of Life
src.quote: Appears four times in Proverbs (3:18; 11:30; 13:12; 15:4), always associated with something that sustains life.
src.note: Proverbs reclaims Eden's image for the wise life.

rec.text: Many advisers -- widely cited as the wisdom rationale for diverse counsel and avoiding echo chambers.
The one who is wise saves lives -- the life-giving vocation of the teacher.

rows.label: Generous and Withholding: The Covenant Economics Paradox
rows.text: One person gives freely yet gains even more; another withholds unduly but comes to poverty. The paradox is the covenant economy: the generous person participates in the divine generosity that sustains all life; the hoarder closes off that participation.

rows.label: Wisdom in Community vs Isolation
rows.text: For lack of guidance a nation falls, but victory is won through many advisers. Wisdom is a social achievement, not a solitary one. The community that refuses counsel collapses; the community that seeks it from multiple directions thrives.

tx.title: Antithetic proverbs — LXX reordering
tx.content: The LXX rearranges several proverbs in chapter 11 compared to MT, moving individual sayings to different positions. This is part of the larger LXX Proverbs phenomenon where the second half of the book diverges dramatically in order from MT. The LXX also adds proverbs not found in any known Hebrew source.
tx.note: The reordering is not random — the LXX translator appears to have grouped thematically related proverbs, suggesting an editorial rather than scribal process.

debate.title: Proverbs 11 and the Prosperity Gospel: Does Generosity Guarantee Material Return?

positions.name: A general principle, not a transactional guarantee
positions.argument: The liberality proverbs (11:24–26) describe God's moral order, not a transactional formula. Generally, generous community members receive more communal support; generally, hoarders lose social capital. This is observation, not promise. The prosperity gospel's transactional version ('give and God must give back materially') distorts Proverbs' observation into a manipulative formula.

positions.name: A theological principle of divine economics
positions.argument: The principle is genuinely theological — God's economy works differently from human calculation. The liberality proverbs are making a claim about how God orders material reality, not merely a sociological observation. The principle is real, even if its mechanism is not always visible and its timing is not always immediate.

positions.name: A wisdom idealisation that experience sometimes contradicts
positions.argument: Proverbs states the principle as generally true; experience sometimes contradicts it. Qohelet (Ecclesiastes) and Job both observe that the generous sometimes lose everything while the hoarders sometimes prosper. The principle needs to be held with epistemic humility.

prayer_prompt: Honest scales please the LORD, but false ones bring His abomination. Consider asking God about your business dealings, your measurements, your honesty in the small weights.
